topic: rain finally came — relief and planting begins

turns.content: the rain has come my friend. finally. I stood in it this morning like a child. hah
Ha! After waiting so long, I don't blame you. There's nothing like the first proper rain after a long dry spell. How heavy was it -- a good soaking or just a teaser?
proper rain. steady from 4am until maybe 9. the ground was drinking. you could hear it. I walked to the farm after and the soil was dark and soft. it is a different world when the water comes

Five hours of steady rain is exactly what the soil needs -- a good slow soak rather than a quick downpour that runs off. When the ground has been dry for a long time, that slow steady rain lets it absorb properly rather than sheeting off the surface.

How are the trees looking after their first real drink?

the older trees you can almost see them waking up. the leaves are lifting already. the young ones I was worried about, they are still alive. some lost leaves in the dry but the stems are green. they will recover Nyame willing

If the stems are green, the trees are alive. They'll put out new leaves within a couple of weeks now that there's moisture. Young cocoa is tough -- it can look dead during dry spells but bounce right back once the rain returns.

Are you planning to get fertilizer down soon while the soil is moist? That's usually the best window.
yes that is the plan. I wait for one or two more rains so the soil is properly settled. then I apply. if I put fertilizer now and the rain stops again for 3 weeks it is wasted. patience my friend

That's smart thinking. Waiting for the rain pattern to establish before applying ensures the fertilizer gets worked into the soil properly. One good rain doesn't guarantee the season has truly started -- you want to see a pattern of regular rainfall before committing your inputs.

Are you also planning to do any new planting this season?

I want to replace some of the old trees that produce less now. maybe 50 trees. I have seedlings ready in the nursery since September. they are strong, good roots. they have been waiting for this rain like all of us

50 replacement trees is a good number to manage. And having them nursery-grown since September means they'll have strong root systems ready to establish quickly. The timing is perfect -- planting in the first weeks of the rainy season gives them the whole wet period to put down roots before the next dry spell.

Plant them in the morning or late afternoon if you can, not midday. Less transplant shock when it's cooler.

yes early morning. before the sun gets strong. I will start planting this week. the children will help on the weekend. even the small one wants to carry seedlings. he is 6 but he thinks he is a farmer already. hah

Ha! At 6 years old, wanting to carry seedlings -- that's how farmers are made. Let him do it. He'll remember planting those trees for the rest of his life. Twenty years from now he'll point to a full-grown cocoa tree and say "I planted that one."

That's a good family day's work. How does the rest of the farm look going into the season?

the drying racks are repaired. fermentation boxes are ready. everything is prepared. now we wait for Nyame to send more rain and the trees to do their work. 'the farmer plants but God waters.' we have done our part my friend

Everything in order and ready to go. You've done the preparation -- repaired the infrastructure, nursed the seedlings, planned the fertilizer timing. Now it's up to the rain and the trees.

Sounds like it could be a good season. Enjoy the planting this week.
I will enjoy it. planting is the best work on the farm. you put something in the ground and you believe it will grow. that is faith my friend. medaase. good night
That's a beautiful way to describe it. Good night, and may the rain keep coming steady. Take care.
